title: Introduction to Psychology
content:
Psychology is the scientific study of mind and behaviour. It explores how people think, feel, perceive, and act — both as individuals and in social groups. The word comes from the Greek psyche (soul/mind) and logos (study).

Modern psychology emerged as a separate discipline in 1879 when Wilhelm Wundt opened the first experimental psychology laboratory in Leipzig, Germany. Before that, questions about the mind were handled by philosophy and physiology.

Major perspectives in psychology each offer a different lens:

* Biological — behaviour stems from genetics, neurons, and brain chemistry
* Psychodynamic — unconscious conflicts and early childhood shape personality (Freud)
* Behavioural — all behaviour is learned through conditioning and reinforcement
* Humanistic — people are motivated by growth and self-actualisation (Maslow, Rogers)
* Cognitive — mental processes like memory, thinking, and problem-solving drive behaviour
* Sociocultural — culture, social norms, and environment influence how we think and act
* Evolutionary — behaviours evolved because they enhanced survival and reproduction

Psychologists use scientific methods: experiments, surveys, case studies, and observations. Ethics are central — participants must give informed consent and must not be harmed.

* Psychology became a science when Wundt applied experimental methods in 1879
* Introspection was Wundt's first method: systematically reporting one's own mental states
* The APA (American Psychological Association) sets ethical standards for research
* Clinical psychologists diagnose and treat mental disorders; psychiatrists can prescribe medication
* Neuroscience and psychology increasingly overlap in the field of neuropsychology
depths:
The history of psychology moves through distinct schools. Structuralism (Wundt, Titchener) tried to break consciousness into basic elements. Functionalism (William James) asked what mental processes do — how they help us adapt. Gestalt psychology argued the whole is greater than the sum of its parts — the brain organises perception into meaningful wholes.

Psychoanalysis (Freud) dominated the early 20th century: unconscious drives, repression, and the id/ego/superego. Behaviourism (Watson, Skinner) rejected the unobservable mind entirely, focusing only on measurable behaviour. The Cognitive Revolution of the 1950s–60s brought mental processes back using information-processing metaphors.

* William James wrote the first major psychology textbook: Principles of Psychology (1890)
* John B. Watson's 'Little Albert' experiment conditioned a baby to fear a white rat
* B.F. Skinner invented the operant conditioning chamber (Skinner box)
* The DSM (Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders) classifies mental conditions
* Positive psychology (Seligman, 2000s) focuses on flourishing rather than disorder
questions: Who opened the first psychology laboratory in 1879?
Wilhelm Wundt founded the first experimental psychology lab in Leipzig in 1879.
Which perspective focuses on unconscious conflicts?
The psychodynamic perspective, rooted in Freud's work, emphasises unconscious processes.
What is the cognitive perspective primarily concerned with?
Mental processes like memory and thinking
Cognitive psychology studies internal mental processes such as memory, perception, and problem-solving.
Psychology became a science by adopting what approach?
Using controlled experiments and empirical methods made psychology a science.
Which perspective argues behaviour is learned through conditioning?
Behaviourism holds that all behaviour is acquired through conditioning and reinforcement.
flashcards: Scientific study of mind and behaviour.
Founded the first psychology lab in 1879; father of experimental psychology.
School holding that psychology should study only observable behaviour.
Focuses on mental processes: memory, thinking, problem-solving.
Emphasises personal growth and self-actualisation (Maslow, Rogers).
Stresses unconscious conflicts and early experiences (Freud).
